questions.text: What is the key difference between a compiler and an interpreter?

options.text: A compiler translates one line at a time; an interpreter translates the whole program first
A compiler translates the whole program before execution; an interpreter translates line by line at runtime
A compiler only works with low-level languages
An interpreter produces a faster executable than a compiler

questions.explanation: A compiler translates the entire source code into machine code before execution, producing an executable. An interpreter translates and runs code line by line at runtime.

questions.text: Which type of error would be caused by writing `WHLIE` instead of `WHILE` in pseudocode?
questions.explanation: A misspelled keyword breaks the grammar rules of the language — this is a syntax error. It would be detected by the compiler or interpreter before the program runs.

questions.text: A program calculates the average of a list but uses division by the wrong variable, producing incorrect results without crashing. What type of error is this?
questions.explanation: A logic error produces incorrect results but doesn't cause a crash. The code is syntactically correct — the algorithm itself is wrong. Only testing can reveal logic errors.

questions.text: What happens during lexical analysis in a compiler?

options.text: Machine code is generated from the parse tree
Source code is broken into tokens and whitespace/comments are removed
The grammar of the code is checked
Variable types are verified for compatibility

questions.explanation: Lexical analysis is the first stage — it tokenises source code (splits it into keywords, identifiers, operators, literals) and strips whitespace and comments.

questions.text: Which SDLC model uses short sprints to deliver working software incrementally?
questions.explanation: Agile divides work into short sprints (1–4 weeks) with working software delivered at the end of each sprint and frequent customer feedback.

questions.text: What is a major weakness of the waterfall model?

options.text: It does not produce any documentation
It is very inflexible — changing requirements late is expensive
It requires too much customer involvement
It does not include a testing phase

questions.explanation: Waterfall is sequential — once a phase is complete it is difficult to go back. If requirements change mid-project, revisions are costly. Testing also happens very late in the process.
An assembler translates assembly language (human-readable mnemonics like MOV and ADD) into machine code (binary instructions). It is a one-to-one translation.

questions.text: What is the main advantage of an interpreter over a compiler during software development?

options.text: It stops at the first error, making debugging easier
It reports all errors at once

questions.explanation: An interpreter stops execution at the first error encountered, which makes it easy to locate and fix bugs one at a time during development.

questions.text: Which good programming principle means code can be easily updated to work with future requirements?
questions.explanation: Extensibility means new features can be added to the program without rewriting existing code. Well-designed, modular programs are typically easier to extend.

questions.text: A company needs to develop software with requirements that are likely to change throughout the project. Which SDLC model is most appropriate?

options.text: Agile — because it is adaptable to changing requirements
Waterfall — because it has a testing phase

questions.explanation: Agile is designed for projects with evolving requirements. Its iterative sprints allow requirements to be refined and new features added throughout the project.

options.text: Requirements that describe what the system must do
Requirements that describe how the system must perform (speed, security, reliability)
Requirements that are not important to the project

questions.explanation: Non-functional requirements describe quality attributes — how the system performs, not what it does. Examples: response time, security level, scalability.
